votes.reason: Python is too permissive and contamination-prone for a clean, auditable tactics-output benchmark.
A purpose-built DSL like Morpeton better matches conditional, prioritized tactical policies than generic data serialization.
Running Morpeton and JSON on identical states/seeds is the only way to make comparisons and attribution defensible.
Separating syntax validity from tactical effectiveness prevents conflating protocol compliance with decision quality.
A formal grammar before implementation is the main safeguard against ambiguity, parser drift, and exploit surfaces.
Partial-parse fallback with public error logging preserves robustness without hiding failures or biasing results.
A 30-line cap is a practical constraint that keeps outputs interpretable and limits prompt-hacking via verbosity.
A small v1 primitive set is sufficient to test conditional tactics while keeping the language learnable and parsable.
First-match-wins with explicit top-to-bottom priority is simple, deterministic, and tests rule ordering competence.
No retry avoids hidden selection effects and cost asymmetries that would contaminate benchmark integrity.
